<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackAllocationSnapshot {
    bool smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring;
    bool largeAllowedWithMonitoring;
    bool guardSucceeded;
    bool allocationBlockedAtLimit;
    bool runtimeAllowedAfterRelease;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate StackMonitor::canAllocate behaviour across modes.
 *
 * @param largeSize Value used for runtime canAllocate after release
 * @return StackAllocationSnapshot Snapshot of allocation checks before/after guard
 */
inline StackAllocationSnapshot test_stack_monitor_capacity(std::size_t largeSize) {
    // Step 1: Disable monitoring
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().setMonitoringEnabled(false);
    
    // Step 2: Initialize stack monitor
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().initialize();
    
    // Step 3: Check allocation without monitoring (small size)
    bool smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(1);
    
    // Step 4: Check allocation without monitoring (large size)
    bool largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // Step 5: Enable monitoring
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().setMonitoringEnabled(true);
    
    // Step 6: Initialize stack monitor again
    reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().initialize();
    
    // Step 7: Check large allocation with monitoring
    bool largeAllowedWithMonitoring = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // Step 8: Create StackGuard in scope block
    bool guardSucceeded = false;
    bool allocationBlockedAtLimit = false;
    {
        constexpr size_t STACK_GUARD_SIZE = SBOConfig::MAX_SBO_STACK_USAGE + 1;
        reaction::StackGuard<STACK_GUARD_SIZE> guard;
        guardSucceeded = guard.success();
        allocationBlockedAtLimit = !guardSucceeded; // If guard failed, allocation was blocked at limit
    } // Guard goes out of scope here
    
    // Step 11: Check runtime allocation after release
    bool runtimeAllowedAfterRelease = reaction::StackMonitor::getInstance().canAllocate(largeSize);
    
    // Step 12: Return stack allocation snapshot
    return StackAllocationSnapshot{
        smallAllowedWithoutMonitoring,
        largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring,
        largeAllowedWithoutMonitoring, // runtimeAllowedWithoutMonitoring (same as large check without monitoring)
        largeAllowedWithMonitoring,
        guardSucceeded,
        allocationBlockedAtLimit,
        runtimeAllowedAfterRelease
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

class TrackingFieldNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    explicit TrackingFieldNode(std::string name) : m_name(name) {}
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override { m_notifications++; }
    int notifications() const { return m_notifications; }
    void clear() { m_notifications = 0; }

private:
    std::string m_name;
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct FieldGraphCleanupResult {
    bool notifiedBeforeDelete;
    bool notifiedAfterDelete;
};

inline FieldGraphCleanupResult test_field_graph_cleanup(uint64_t objectId) {
    // Step 1: Create owner and field TrackingFieldNode instances
    auto owner = std::make_shared<TrackingFieldNode>("owner");
    auto field = std::make_shared<TrackingFieldNode>("field");
    
    // Step 2: Get observer graph instance
    auto& observerGraph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 3: Register nodes
    observerGraph.addNode(owner);
    observerGraph.addNode(field);
    
    // Step 4: Get field graph instance
    auto& fieldGraph = reaction::FieldGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 5: Register field nodes under object ID
    fieldGraph.addObjField(objectId, field->shared_from_this());
    
    // Step 6: Bind field to owner
    observerGraph.addObserver(owner, field);
    
    // Step 7: Trigger notification on field
    field->valueChanged();
    
    // Step 8: Check if owner was notified before delete
    bool notifiedBefore = owner->notifications() > 0;
    
    // Step 9: Clear owner notification count
    owner->clear();
    
    // Step 10: Clear dependencies on owner
    observerGraph.clearNodeDependents(owner);
    
    // Step 11: Remove field mappings for object ID
    fieldGraph.deleteObjField(objectId);
    
    // Step 12: Attempt to bind field again
    observerGraph.addObserver(owner, field); // Re-bind
    
    // Step 13: Trigger notification on field again
    field->valueChanged();
    
    // Step 14: Check if owner was notified after delete
    bool notifiedAfter = owner->notifications() > 0;
    
    // Step 15: Close nodes
    observerGraph.closeNode(owner);
    observerGraph.closeNode(field);
    
    // Step 16: Return field graph cleanup result
    return FieldGraphCleanupResult{notifiedBefore, notifiedAfter};
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/memory/memory_config.h>
#include <reaction/memory/stack_monitor.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct StackLimitSnapshot {
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::UsageStats statsBeforeGuard;
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::UsageStats statsAfterGuard;
    bool templateAllocationAllowed;
    bool runtimeAllocationAllowed;
    bool guardHeld;
    bool allocationBlockedWhileGuardHeld;
};

StackLimitSnapshot test_stack_monitor_limits() {
    // Step 1: Enable monitoring
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::enableMonitoring();
    
    // Step 2: Initialize stack monitor
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::initialize();
    
    // Step 3: Get usage statistics before guard
    auto statsBeforeGuard = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::getStats();
    
    // Step 4: Check if template allocation is allowed
    struct LargeType { char buffer[1024 * 1024] }; // 1MB type
    bool templateAllocationAllowed = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate<LargeType>();
    
    // Step 5: Check if runtime allocation is allowed
    bool runtimeAllocationAllowed = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(1024 * 1024);
    
    // Step 6: Create large StackGuard instance
    reaction::memory::StackMonitor::StackGuard<1024 * 1024> guard;
    
    // Step 7: Check if guard succeeded
    bool guardHeld = guard.success();
    
    // Step 8: Check if allocation blocked while guard is held
    bool allocationBlockedWhileGuardHeld = !reaction::memory::StackMonitor::canAllocate(1);
    
    // Step 9: Get usage statistics after guard
    auto statsAfterGuard = reaction::memory::StackMonitor::getStats();
    
    // Step 10: Return stack limit snapshot
    return StackLimitSnapshot{
        .statsBeforeGuard = statsBeforeGuard,
        .statsAfterGuard = statsAfterGuard,
        .templateAllocationAllowed = templateAllocationAllowed,
        .runtimeAllocationAllowed = runtimeAllocationAllowed,
        .guardHeld = guardHeld,
        .allocationBlockedWhileGuardHeld = allocationBlockedWhileGuardHeld
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/resource.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>
#include <string>
#include <type_traits>

namespace test_reaction {

struct VoidResourceSnapshot {
    bool retrievedIsVoid;
    bool usesSboStorage;
    bool nameAssigned;
    bool nameClearedAfterClose;
};

inline VoidResourceSnapshot test_resource_void_behavior(const std::string &nodeName) {
    // 1. Create shared Resource<Void> instance
    auto resource = std::make_shared<reaction::Resource<Void>>();
    
    // 2. Check if resource uses SBO storage
    bool uses_sbo = resource->isUsingSBO();
    
    // 3. Get value from resource
    auto value = resource->getValue();
    
    // 4. Check if retrieved value is Void type
    bool is_void = std::is_same_v<decltype(value), reaction::Void>;
    
    // 5. Get observer graph instance
    auto &observer_graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // 6. Register resource node
    observer_graph.addNode(resource);
    
    // 7. Assign node name
    observer_graph.setName(resource, nodeName);
    
    // 8. Check if name was assigned
    bool name_assigned = (observer_graph.getName(resource) == nodeName);
    
    // 9. Close node
    observer_graph.closeNode(resource);
    
    // 10. Check if name was cleared after close
    bool name_cleared = (observer_graph.getName(resource) == "");
    
    // 11. Return void resource snapshot
    return VoidResourceSnapshot{
        .retrievedIsVoid = is_void,
        .usesSboStorage = uses_sbo,
        .nameAssigned = name_assigned,
        .nameClearedAfterClose = name_cleared
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

 The function must perform a series of steps involving creating nodes, attaching observers, triggering notifications, and rewiring dependencies.

 Steps:

 7. Record the notification count after the initial setup.
 8. Rewire dependencies: detach the CountingObserver from the first source and attach it to the second source.
 9. Trigger a notification on the first source (old source) and record the notification count.
10. Trigger a notification on the second source (new source) and record the notification count.
11. Trigger a notification on the third source and record the notification count.
12. Close all nodes.
13. Return a snapshot of the observer node links (which includes the recorded counts).

 We are provided with the structure `ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot` that holds four integers.

 Implementation notes:
 - We assume that the `reaction` library is included and available.
 - We use the `reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance()` to get the graph.
 - We use `registerNode` to register each node.
 - We use `attach` to attach the observer to a source.
 - We use `valueChanged` to trigger notifications (by calling it on the source nodes).
 - We use `notifications` to get the count from the CountingObserver.
 - We use `closeNode` to close each node.

 However, note that the `valueChanged` method in the CountingObserver is overridden and increments the notification count.

 Let's break down the code step by step.

 Important: The function `test_observer_node_links` must return an `ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot`.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/observer_graph.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

class CountingObserver : public reaction::ObserverNode {
public:
    void valueChanged(bool changed = true) override {
        m_notifications++;
    }
    
    int notifications() const {
        return m_notifications;
    }

private:
    int m_notifications = 0;
};

struct ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot {
    int firstNotificationsAfterInitial;
    int firstNotificationsAfterUpdate;
    int secondNotificationsAfterUpdate;
    int thirdNotificationsAfterUpdate;
};

ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot test_observer_node_links() {
    // Step 1: Create CountingObserver instance
    auto countingObserver = std::make_shared<CountingObserver>();
    
    // Step 2: Create three source ObserverNode instances
    auto source1 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    auto source2 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    auto source3 = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    
    // Step 3: Get observer graph instance
    auto& graph = reaction::ObserverGraph::getInstance();
    
    // Step 4: Register all nodes
    graph.registerNode(countingObserver);
    graph.registerNode(source1);
    graph.registerNode(source2);
    graph.registerNode(source3);
    
    // Step 5: Attach single observer (countingObserver) to first source
    source1->attach(countingObserver);
    
    // Step 6: Trigger notification on first source
    source1->notify();
    
    // Step 7: Get notification count after initial setup
    int firstAfterInitial = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 8: Rewire dependencies - detach from source1 and attach to source2
    source1->detach(countingObserver);
    source2->attach(countingObserver);
    
    // Step 9: Trigger notification on old source (source1)
    source1->notify();
    
    // Step 10: Get notification count after old source notification
    int firstAfterUpdate = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 11: Trigger notification on second source (source2)
    source2->notify();
    
    // Step 12: Get notification count after second source notification
    int secondAfterUpdate = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 13: Trigger notification on third source (source3)
    source3->notify();
    
    // Step 14: Get notification count after third source notification
    int thirdAfterUpdate = countingObserver->notifications();
    
    // Step 15: Close all nodes
    countingObserver->closeNode();
    source1->closeNode();
    source2->closeNode();
    source3->closeNode();
    
    // Step 16: Return observer node link snapshot
    return ObserverNodeLinkSnapshot{
        firstAfterInitial,
        firstAfterUpdate,
        secondAfterUpdate,
        thirdAfterUpdate
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <cstdint>

namespace test_reaction {

struct AtomicCompoundSnapshot {
    int afterAddition;
    int afterMultiplication;
    int afterBitwiseAnd;
    int afterShift;
    int postIncrementReturn;
    int finalValue;
    int calcEvaluations;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate atomic compound assignments on a reactive counter.
 *
 * @return AtomicCompoundSnapshot Snapshot with values after each compound operation.
 */
inline AtomicCompoundSnapshot test_atomic_compound_assign() {
    // Create reactive variable initialized to 0
    auto reactive_var = reaction::var(0);
    
    // Create counter to track calculation evaluations
    auto evaluation_counter = reaction::var(0);
    
    // Create calculation that tracks value and increments evaluation counter
    auto calc_value = reaction::calc([&]() {
        evaluation_counter++;
        return reactive_var();
    });
    
    // Apply atomic addition (e.g., add 5)
    reactive_var.atomicAddAssign(5);
    int after_addition = calc_value();
    
    // Apply atomic multiplication (e.g., multiply by 3)
    reactive_var.atomicMulAssign(3);
    int after_multiplication = calc_value();
    
    // Apply atomic bitwise AND (e.g., AND with 0x0F)
    reactive_var.atomicBitwiseAndAssign(0x0F);
    int after_bitwise_and = calc_value();
    
    // Apply atomic left shift (e.g., left shift by 2)
    reactive_var.atomicLeftShiftAssign(2);
    int after_shift = calc_value();
    
    // Apply post-increment
    int post_increment_return = reactive_var.postIncrement();
    int final_value = reactive_var();
    
    // Get final evaluation count
    int calc_evaluations = evaluation_counter();
    
    return AtomicCompoundSnapshot{
        after_addition,
        after_multiplication,
        after_bitwise_and,
        after_shift,
        post_increment_return,
        final_value,
        calc_evaluations
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <reaction/graph/batch.h>
#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

struct DepthOrderingSnapshot {
    bool initialComparatorResult;
    bool shallowBeforeDeep;
    bool deepBeforeShallow;
    bool nullComesFirst;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate ObserverNode::updateDepth cooperating with BatchCompare ordering.
 *
 * @return DepthOrderingSnapshot Comparator outcomes after adjusting node depths.
 */
inline DepthOrderingSnapshot test_observer_depth_ordering() {
    // Step 1: Define DepthProbeNode class
    class DepthProbeNode : public reaction::ObserverNode {
    public:
        void setDepth(uint16_t depth) {
            this->updateDepth(depth);
        }
    };

    // Step 2: Get thread manager instance
    auto& tm = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // Step 3: Reset thread manager state
    tm.resetForTesting();
    
    // Step 4: Enable thread safety
    tm.enableThreadSafety();
    
    // Step 5: Create shallow and deep DepthProbeNode instances
    auto shallowNode = std::make_shared<DepthProbeNode>();
    auto deepNode = std::make_shared<DepthProbeNode>();
    
    // Step 6: Create NodeWeak from nodes
    auto shallowWeak = shallowNode->getPtr();
    auto deepWeak = deepNode->getPtr();
    
    // Step 7: Create BatchCompare instance
    reaction::BatchCompare batchCompare;
    
    // Step 8: Compare initial nodes (depth 0)
    bool initialResult = batchCompare(shallowWeak, deepWeak);
    
    // Step 9: Update depths of nodes
    shallowNode->setDepth(5);  // Shallow depth
    deepNode->setDepth(10);   // Deep depth
    
    // Step 10: Compare shallow before deep
    bool shallowBefore = batchCompare(shallowWeak, deepWeak);
    
    // Step 11: Update depth of shallow node again
    shallowNode->setDepth(15); // Shallow now deeper
    
    // Step 12: Compare deep before shallow
    bool deepBefore = batchCompare(deepWeak, shallowWeak);
    
    // Step 13: Create null NodeWeak
    reaction::NodeWeak nullWeak; // Null weak pointer
    
    // Step 14: Compare null with shallow
    bool nullFirst = batchCompare(nullWeak, shallowWeak);
    
    // Step 15: Return depth ordering snapshot
    return DepthOrderingSnapshot{
        .initialComparatorResult = initialResult,
        .shallowBeforeDeep = shallowBefore,
        .deepBeforeShallow = deepBefore,
        .nullComesFirst = nullFirst
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct UnaryExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    bool logicalNotAfterFlip;
    int bitwiseNotValue;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate unary expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function showcases how low-level expression builder helpers (`make_binary_op`,
 * `make_unary_op`) and high-level unary operators (`-`, `!`, `~`) cooperate with the
 * `expr` factory to produce reactive expressions.
 *
 * @param a First input value participating in the sum expression
 * @param b Second input value participating in the sum expression
 * @return UnaryExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of expression outputs before/after updates
 */
inline UnaryExpressionSnapshot test_unary_expression_builders(int a, int b) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto var1 = reaction::var(a);
    auto var2 = reaction::var(b);
    auto boolVar = reaction::var(true);
    auto intVar = reaction::var(5);

    // Build negated sum expression
    auto sumExpr = var1 + var2;
    auto negSum = -sumExpr;

    // Build logical NOT expression
    auto notExpr = !boolVar;

    // Build bitwise NOT expression
    auto bitNotExpr = ~intVar;

    // Get initial values
    int initialNegatedSum = negSum.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    var1.value(a + 1);
    var2.value(b + 1);
    boolVar.value(false);
    intVar.value(10);

    // Get updated values
    int updatedNegatedSum = negSum.get();
    bool logicalNotAfterFlip = notExpr.get();
    int bitwiseNotValue = bitNotExpr.get();

    return UnaryExpressionSnapshot{
        .initialNegatedSum = initialNegatedSum,
        .updatedNegatedSum = updatedNegatedSum,
        .logicalNotAfterFlip = logicalNotAfterFlip,
        .bitwiseNotValue = bitwiseNotValue
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>

namespace test_reaction {

struct LogicalExpressionSnapshot {
    bool initialAnd;
    bool initialOr;
    bool updatedAnd;
    bool updatedOr;
};

/**
 * Function: Demonstrate logical expression builders and operators on reactive values.
 *
 * This function shows how high-level logical operators (`&&`, `||`) compose reactive
 * expressions that are then lifted into `expr` calculations.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first boolean input
 * @param b Initial value for the second boolean input
 * @param c Initial value for the fallback boolean input
 * @return LogicalExpressionSnapshot Snapshot of AND/OR results before and after updates
 */
inline LogicalExpressionSnapshot test_logical_expression_builders(bool a, bool b, bool c) {
    // Create reactive boolean variables
    auto varA = reaction::var(a);
    auto varB = reaction::var(b);
    auto varC = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Build logical expressions
    auto andExpr = varA && varB;
    auto orExpr = varA || varB;
    
    // Get initial results
    bool initialAnd = andExpr.get();
    bool initialOr = orExpr.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    varA.value(!a);  // Flip a
    varB.value(!b);  // Flip b
    varC.value(true); // Change c (though it's not used in expressions)
    
    // Get updated results
    bool updatedAnd = andExpr.get();
    bool updatedOr = orExpr.get();
    
    return LogicalExpressionSnapshot{
        initialAnd,
        initialOr,
        updatedAnd,
        updatedOr
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/cache/graph_cache.h>
#include <reaction/core/observer_node.h>
#include <memory>

namespace test_reaction {

struct NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterCache;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool metricsCachedExists;
    bool metricsClearedAfterInvalidate;
};

NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot test_node_metrics_cache() {
    // Step 1: Create NodeMetricsCache instance
    auto cache = std::make_shared<reaction::NodeMetricsCache>();
    
    // Step 2: Create ObserverNode instance
    auto node = std::make_shared<reaction::ObserverNode>();
    
    // Step 3: Get cache statistics before caching
    auto statsBefore = cache->getStats();
    
    // Step 4: Query metrics before caching
    auto metricsBefore = cache->getNodeMetrics(node);
    
    // Step 5: Cache node metrics
    cache->cacheNodeMetrics(node, reaction::NodeMetrics{true, 0, 0, 0});
    
    // Step 6: Query metrics after caching
    auto metricsAfter = cache->getNodeMetrics(node);
    
    // Step 7: Get cache statistics after caching
    auto statsAfter = cache->getStats();
    
    // Step 8: Invalidate all entries
    cache->invalidateAll();
    
    // Step 9: Query metrics after invalidation
    auto metricsInvalidated = cache->getNodeMetrics(node);
    
    // Step 10: Get cache statistics after invalidation
    auto statsInvalidated = cache->getStats();
    
    // Step 11: Return node metrics cache snapshot
    return NodeMetricsCacheSnapshot{
        .entriesBefore = statsBefore.totalEntries,
        .entriesAfterCache = statsAfter.totalEntries,
        .entriesAfterInvalidate = statsInvalidated.totalEntries,
        .hitCount = statsInvalidated.hitCount,
        .missCount = statsInvalidated.missCount,
        .hitRatio = statsInvalidated.hitRatio,
        .metricsCachedExists = (metricsAfter != nullptr) && metricsAfter->exists,
        .metricsClearedAfterInvalidate = (metricsInvalidated == nullptr)
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

#pragma once

#include <reaction/cache/cache_base.h>
#include <memory>
#include <chrono>

namespace test_reaction {

struct GenericCacheStatsSnapshot {
    std::size_t entriesBefore;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert;
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate;
    std::size_t hitCount;
    std::size_t missCount;
    double hitRatio;
    bool firstLookupWasMiss;
    bool secondLookupWasHit;
};

GenericCacheStatsSnapshot test_generic_cache_base() {
    // Define IntCache class inheriting from CacheBase
    class IntCache : public reaction::CacheBase<int, int> {
    public:
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::CacheBase;
        
        // Expose CacheBase methods
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::getStats;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::getCachedValue;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::cacheValue;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::invalidateAllEntries;
        using reaction::CacheBase<int, int>::triggerCleanup;
    };

    std::size_t entriesBefore = initialStats.totalEntries;

    const int* firstValue = cache.getCachedValue(42);
    bool firstLookupWasMiss = (firstValue == nullptr);
    
    // Insert value into cache
    cache.cacheValue(42, 100);
    
    // Perform second lookup (should be hit)
    const int* secondValue = cache.getCachedValue(42);
    bool secondLookupWasHit = (secondValue != nullptr);
    
    // Get stats after insertion
    auto afterInsertStats = cache.getStats();
    std::size_t entriesAfterInsert = afterInsertStats.totalEntries;
    
    // Invalidate all entries
    cache.invalidateAllEntries();
    
    // Trigger cleanup
    cache.triggerCleanup();
    
    // Get stats after invalidation
    auto afterInvalidateStats = cache.getStats();
    std::size_t entriesAfterInvalidate = afterInvalidateStats.totalEntries;
    
    // Return snapshot
    return GenericCacheStatsSnapshot{
        .entriesBefore = entriesBefore,
        .entriesAfterInsert = entriesAfterInsert,
        .entriesAfterInvalidate = entriesAfterInvalidate,
        .hitCount = afterInsertStats.hitCount,
        .missCount = afterInsertStats.missCount,
        .hitRatio = afterInsertStats.hitRatio,
        .firstLookupWasMiss = firstLookupWasMiss,
        .secondLookupWasHit = secondLookupWasHit
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>We are given the function signature and a struct to return.
 We need to implement the function using the reaction library.

 Steps:
 1. Get the thread manager instance (using reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance())
 2. Reset the thread manager state (using resetForTesting() on the instance)
 3. Check thread safety before any registration (using isThreadSafetyEnabled() on the instance)
 4. Register the main thread (using registerThread() on the instance)
 5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
 6. Create a worker thread and register it (we'll use std::thread for this)
 7. Wait for the worker thread to complete
 8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
 9. Return a ForceRegisterSnapshot with the three boolean values.

 Note: The function is in the namespace test_reaction.

 Let's write the code accordingly.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/concurrency/thread_manager.h>
#include <thread>
#include <memory>
#include <shared_mutex>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *
 * Collects observable state around calls to ThreadManager registration APIs.
 */
struct ForceRegisterSnapshot {
    bool safetyBeforeForce;
    bool safetyAfterMainForce;
    bool safetyAfterWorkerForce;
};

/**
 * Function: test_thread_manager_force_register
 *
 * Demonstrates how registering multiple distinct threads causes
 * ThreadManager to enable thread safety.
 *
 * - We start from a clean ThreadManager state.
 * - The main thread registers itself and remains the sole thread.
 * - A worker thread then also registers, which should trigger
 *   multi-thread detection and enable thread safety.
 *
 * @return ForceRegisterSnapshot
 *         A snapshot of the thread-safety flag before any registration,
 *         after the main-thread registration, and after the worker-thread registration.
 */
inline ForceRegisterSnapshot test_thread_manager_force_register() {
    ForceRegisterSnapshot snapshot;
    
    // 1. Get thread manager instance
    auto& thread_manager = reaction::ThreadManager::getInstance();
    
    // 2. Reset thread manager state
    thread_manager.resetForTesting();
    
    // 3. Check thread safety before registration
    snapshot.safetyBeforeForce = thread_manager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 4. Register main thread
    thread_manager.registerThread();
    
    // 5. Check thread safety after main thread registration
    snapshot.safetyAfterMainForce = thread_manager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    // 6. Create worker thread and register it
    std::thread worker_thread([&thread_manager]() {
        thread_manager.registerThread();
    });
    
    // 7. Wait for worker thread to complete
    worker_thread.join();
    
    // 8. Check thread safety after worker thread registration
    snapshot.safetyAfterWorkerForce = thread_manager.isThreadSafetyEnabled();
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

/**

struct RawExpressionSnapshot {

};

/**
 * Function: test_raw_expression_builders
 *
 * Demonstrates how low-level expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) can be combined with
 * `expr` to build reactive calculations without using the higher
 * level `make_binary_op` / `make_unary_op` shortcuts.
 *
 * The function:
 * - Creates three reactive integers A, B, C.
 * - Builds sum = A + B via make_binary_expr.
 * - Builds negated = -(A + B) via make_unary_expr(make_binary_expr(...)).
 * - Builds chained = -(A + B) * C via nested make_* calls.
 * - Updates A and C and records all expression values before and after.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first input
 * @param b Initial value for the second input
 * @param c Initial value for the chaining multiplier
 * @return RawExpressionSnapshot
 *         Snapshot of sum / negated / chained values before and after updates.
 */
inline RawExpressionSnapshot test_raw_expression_builders(int a, int b, int c);

}  // namespace test_reaction
```

 Steps in code:

 1. Create three reactive variables: A, B, C using `reaction::var(a)`, etc.
 2. Build the sum expression: `sum_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::AddOp>(A, B)`
 3. Build the negated expression: `negated_expr = reaction::make_unary_expr<reaction::NegOp>(sum_expr)`
 4. Build the chained expression: `chained_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::MulOp>(negated_expr, C)`
 5. Convert these expressions to reactive expressions (using `reaction::expr`) so they become reactive and can be observed.
 6. Use `get()` to get the initial values of the reactive expressions.
 7. Update the variables (A and C) using `value(new_value)` method on the reactive variables.
 8. Get the updated values.

 However, note that the expressions are built using the raw builders and then wrapped in `expr` to make them reactive.

 Important: The function returns a `RawExpressionSnapshot` which has initial and updated values for sum, negated sum, and chained.

 Let's write the code accordingly.

 Note: The `reaction::expr` function is used to create a reactive expression from an expression object (like the ones built by `make_binary_expr` and `make_unary_expr`). This makes the expression reactive and allows it to update when its dependencies change.

 We assume that the reactive variables are of type `React` (or similar) and that we can call `value` to update them.

 Implementation:

 We'll create the variables, then the expressions, then get the initial values, then update A and C, then get the updated values.

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/reaction.h>
#include <reaction/expression/expression_builders.h>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: RawExpressionSnapshot
 *
 * Captures the values produced by raw expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) before and after updating
 * their reactive inputs.
 */
struct RawExpressionSnapshot {
    int initialSum;
    int initialNegatedSum;
    int initialChainedValue;

    int updatedSum;
    int updatedNegatedSum;
    int updatedChainedValue;
};

/**
 * Function: test_raw_expression_builders
 *
 * Demonstrates how low-level expression builder helpers
 * (`make_binary_expr`, `make_unary_expr`) can be combined with
 * `expr` to build reactive calculations without using the higher
 * level `make_binary_op` / `make_unary_op` shortcuts.
 *
 * The function:
 * - Creates three reactive integers A, B, C.
 * - Builds sum = A + B via make_binary_expr.
 * - Builds negated = -(A + B) via make_unary_expr(make_binary_expr(...)).
 * - Builds chained = -(A + B) * C via nested make_* calls.
 * - Updates A and C and records all expression values before and after.
 *
 * @param a Initial value for the first input
 * @param b Initial value for the second input
 * @param c Initial value for the chaining multiplier
 * @return RawExpressionSnapshot
 *         Snapshot of sum / negated / chained values before and after updates.
 */
inline RawExpressionSnapshot test_raw_expression_builders(int a, int b, int c) {
    // Create reactive variables
    auto A = reaction::var(a);
    auto B = reaction::var(b);
    auto C = reaction::var(c);
    
    // Build sum expression: A + B
    auto sum_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::AddOp>(A, B);
    auto sum_reactive = reaction::expr(sum_expr);
    
    // Build negated expression: -(A + B)
    auto negated_expr = reaction::make_unary_expr<reaction::NegOp>(sum_expr);
    auto negated_reactive = reaction::expr(negated_expr);
    
    // Build chained expression: -(A + B) * C
    auto chained_expr = reaction::make_binary_expr<reaction::MulOp>(negated_expr, C);
    auto chained_reactive = reaction::expr(chained_expr);
    
    // Get initial values
    RawExpressionSnapshot snapshot;
    snapshot.initialSum = sum_reactive.get();
    snapshot.initialNegatedSum = negated_reactive.get();
    snapshot.initialChainedValue = chained_reactive.get();
    
    // Update variables to trigger recomputation
    A.value(a + 10);  // Update A
    C.value(c + 20);  // Update C
    
    // Get updated values
    snapshot.updatedSum = sum_reactive.get();
    snapshot.updatedNegatedSum = negated_reactive.get();
    snapshot.updatedChainedValue = chained_reactive.get();
    
    return snapshot;
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#pragma once

#include <reaction/expression/atomic_operations.h>
#include <cstdint>

namespace test_reaction {

/**
 * Struct: AtomicHelperSnapshot
 *
 * Records the value of an integer after a sequence of atomic compound helper
 * operations and whether each operation reported a change.
 */
struct AtomicHelperSnapshot {
    int32_t finalValue;
    bool addReportedChange;
    bool multiplyReportedChange;
    bool andReportedChange;
    bool leftShiftReportedChange;
    bool incrementReportedChange;
};

/**
 * Helper implementation type that mimics the minimal interface required by
 * reaction::atomicCompoundAssign helpers. It stores an integer and exposes
 * an atomicOperation method that receives a lambda and tracks whether the
 * value was changed.
 */
class IntAtomicImpl {
public:
    explicit IntAtomicImpl(int32_t initial) : m_value(initial), m_lastReportedChange(false) {}
    
    template <typename F>
    void atomicOperation(F&& op) {
        bool changed = true;
        {
            // Simulate atomic operation without locking for simplicity
            try {
                changed = op(m_value);
            } catch (...) {
                changed = false;
            }
        }
        m_lastReportedChange = changed;
    }
    
    template <typename F>
    void atomicOperation(F&& op, bool forceNotify) {
        bool changed = true;
        {
            // Simulate atomic operation without locking for simplicity
            try {
                changed = op(m_value);
            } catch (...) {
                changed = false;
            }
        }
        m_lastReportedChange = changed || forceNotify;
    }
    
    int32_t value() const {
        return m_value;
    }
    
    bool lastReportedChange() const {
        return m_lastReportedChange;
    }
    
private:
    int32_t m_value;
    bool m_lastReportedChange;
};

/**
 * Function: test_atomic_compound_helpers
 *
 * Demonstrates the low-level atomic compound helper APIs:
 * - atomicCompoundAssign
 * - atomicAddAssign
 * - atomicMultiplyAssign
 * - atomicBitwiseAndAssign
 * - atomicLeftShiftAssign
 * - atomicIncrement
 *
 * These helpers operate on a simple IntAtomicImpl type instead of a full
 * React wrapper, making it easy to observe value and change reporting.
 *
 * @param initial Initial integer value
 * @return AtomicHelperSnapshot
 *         Snapshot containing the final value and which operations reported
 *         that a change occurred.
 */
inline AtomicHelperSnapshot test_atomic_compound_helpers(int32_t initial) {
    IntAtomicImpl atomicImpl(initial);
    
    // Test atomic add assignment
    reaction::atomicAddAssign(atomicImpl, 5);
    bool addReported = atomicImpl.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomic multiply assignment
    reaction::atomicMultiplyAssign(atomicImpl, 3);
    bool multiplyReported = atomicImpl.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomic bitwise AND assignment
    reaction::atomicBitwiseAndAssign(atomicImpl, 0x0F);
    bool andReported = atomicImpl.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomic left shift assignment
    reaction::atomicLeftShiftAssign(atomicImpl, 2);
    bool leftShiftReported = atomicImpl.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Test atomic increment
    reaction::atomicIncrement(atomicImpl);
    bool incrementReported = atomicImpl.lastReportedChange();
    
    // Get final value
    int32_t finalValue = atomicImpl.value();
    
    return AtomicHelperSnapshot{
        .finalValue = finalValue,
        .addReportedChange = addReported,
        .multiplyReportedChange = multiplyReported,
        .andReportedChange = andReported,
        .leftShiftReportedChange = leftShiftReported,
        .incrementReportedChange = incrementReported
    };
}

}  // namespace test_reaction
```</answer>
